title: Agentic Architecture for Vibe Founders
hook: The 5-layer questionnaire system, dual-model routing (smart brain plus cheap hands), parallel task fan-out, and a 90-day build plan for solo founders shipping agentic AI products in 2026. Builder-to-builder, no enterprise pretensions.
benefits: The vibe founder vs enterprise architect frame. Why most agentic AI content is written for 50-engineer teams that do not exist. The 5 design philosophies that flip when you ship solo: build small, ship in days not quarters, boring infrastructure, route by cost, user as co-author.

The 5-Layer Questionnaire Architecture in full. Layer 1 captures intent (2 to 3 questions). Layer 2 surfaces unknowns. Layer 3 proposes answers pre-filled from web research and asks the user to confirm. Layer 4 locks context into one prompt block. Layer 5 executes. Worked example: a SaaS launch.

Dual model routing. Smart brain plus cheap hands. Use Claude Sonnet or GPT-4.5 for planning, structure, and judgement. Use Gemini Flash or GPT-mini for API payloads, format conversions, and boring transforms. The 3-line decision rule and a 200-task batch cost-math showing 80 percent savings.

Parallel task fan-out. When the context is locked, fan out 200 tasks concurrently instead of sequentially. Concurrency caps that respect API rate limits. Exponential backoff retry with a 3-attempt ceiling. Partial-failure handling so you ship the wins and queue the misses for a clean second pass.

State, storage, and resumability. Why agentic systems must survive a crash mid-batch. The 3-tier storage model: in-memory for current task, JSONL for within-session resume, persistent DB for across-session resume. Worked example: a 200-task batch where task 74 fails and the system picks up at 75.

The vibe founder's toolkit. Claude Sonnet for planning, Haiku for routing, Gemini Flash for parallel payloads, a small Node.js or Python orchestrator (no framework), JSONL for state, Railway for deployment. Operational cost under $50 a month at 100k tasks. Why LangChain is over-engineered here.

The 90-day build plan, week by week. Week 1 specs the questionnaire layers. Weeks 2 to 3 build the dual-model router. Weeks 4 to 6 ship parallel fan-out plus state. Weeks 7 to 9 run a production workload with one paying client. Weeks 10 to 13 iterate on what broke. Closing 3-line ship-it call.
squeezeBullets: 5-layer questionnaire architecture, dual model routing for 80 percent cost savings, parallel fan-out for 200 tasks, full state model
The vibe founder's toolkit: Claude for thinking, Gemini Flash for payloads, JSONL state, Railway deploy, under $50 a month at 100k tasks
90-day build plan, week by week, with worked examples and the 3 line ship-it call at the end
description:
Most content tagged agentic AI is written by people who have never shipped a working agent. They build for engineering teams of 50 that exist in 4 companies in the world. Vibe founders need a different playbook. You ship in days, not quarters. You pick boring infrastructure that does not need a Kubernetes cluster. You route every task by cost-per-token before you route by anything else. And you treat the user as a co-author of the spec, not a downstream consumer of it.

This playbook is the architecture I keep coming back to whenever I build a new agentic product as a solo founder. Seven parts, builder-to-builder, slightly more technical than my marketing playbooks. No theory padding. The worked example throughout is a launch-kit generator for a new SaaS, where one founder runs the questionnaire once and the system fans out 200 tasks (cover image, landing copy, ads, emails, social posts) in parallel.

Part 1, The Vibe Founder vs Enterprise Architect. The frame that makes the rest of this playbook usable. The 5 design philosophies that change when you are shipping solo. Why the books, the talks, and the hot-takes from people running agent platforms at 200-engineer companies are mostly noise for your stack. The shape of an agentic product a single founder can actually own.

Part 2, The 5-Layer Questionnaire Architecture. The core idea. Most agentic systems fail because they assume the user can write a perfect prompt. They cannot. The 5-layer questionnaire walks the user from raw intent to locked context across 5 progressively deeper passes. Layer 1: capture intent in 2 to 3 questions. Layer 2: surface unknowns the system needs. Layer 3: propose answers pre-filled from web research and ask the user to confirm or edit. Layer 4: lock combined context into a single system-prompt block. Layer 5: execute. Worked example: a SaaS founder runs the layers and the system has everything it needs to ship a 200-asset launch kit by the end of Layer 4.

Part 3, Dual Model Routing. Smart Brain plus Cheap Hands. The cost-quality split that pays the rent. Use the smart model (Claude Sonnet, GPT-4.5, Opus) for planning, structure decisions, and judgement. Use the cheap model (Gemini Flash, GPT-mini, Haiku) for API payload generation, format conversions, JSON shaping, and the repetitive work. The 3-line decision rule you can paste into a routing function. Worked cost math: a 200-task batch routed naively costs $14, routed with the dual split costs $2.40. That is 83 percent saved on the same workload.

Part 4, Parallel Task Fan-Out. When the locked context is ready and you have 200 things to ship, you fan out concurrently, not sequentially. The default concurrency cap (start at 8 to 12 concurrent workers and climb from there). Rate-limit handling so you do not hammer the API provider into a soft block. Exponential backoff retry with a 3-attempt ceiling. Partial-failure handling: when 184 of 200 tasks succeed, you ship the wins immediately and queue the 16 misses. The pattern that turns a 90-minute serial run into an 8-minute parallel run.

Part 5, State, Storage, and Resumability. Agentic systems crash. Network blips, rate limits, the model returning malformed JSON, your laptop's lid closing. The system that cannot survive a crash mid-batch is a toy. The 3-tier storage model: in-memory for the current task, JSONL appended on disk for within-session resume, persistent database (Postgres or SQLite) for across-session resume. When to use each tier. Worked example: a 200-task batch where task 74 errors out, the system flushes state, restarts, and picks up at task 75 instead of replaying 1 through 73.

Part 6, The Vibe Founder's Toolkit. The actual stack. Claude Sonnet for planning, Claude Haiku for the cheap routing decision itself, Gemini Flash for parallel payload generation, a small Node.js or Python orchestrator written in one file (no framework), JSONL on disk for state, Postgres on Supabase for the database, Railway for deployment, S3 for any binary outputs. Operational cost: under $50 a month at 100k tasks. Why LangChain, LlamaIndex, and the agent-framework ecosystem is overkill at this scale, and what you give up by skipping it (mostly nothing you needed).

Part 7, The 90-Day Build Plan. The week-by-week. Week 1: spec the questionnaire layers for your specific use case. Weeks 2 to 3: build the dual-model router and prove the cost math on a small workload. Weeks 4 to 6: ship parallel fan-out plus state, run a synthetic 200-task batch end to end. Weeks 7 to 9: bring on one paying client, run their first production workload, write down what broke. Weeks 10 to 13: iterate on what broke, ship v1 to the next 5 clients. Closing 3-line ship-it call.

Included as a printable PDF and a duplicatable Notion reference. The GitHub repo at github.com/AI-BrandFactory/agentic-architecture-vibe-founders ships the playbook plus reference snippets. Built to be opened on a Tuesday morning and used to spec your first agentic product by Friday.
emailSubject: Your Agentic Architecture for Vibe Founders playbook is ready
